---
title: "TAMBAPANNI: A History Without Borders"
subtitle: "Reconstructing the Identity of the Island Known Today as Sri Lanka"
date: 2026-03-01
author:
  - name: Ananthaneshan Elampoornan
    orcid: 0009-0004-9181-2270
google-scholar: true
citation:
  type: article
  container-title: "Tambapanni White Paper Series"
  issued: 2026-03
  url: https://tambapanni.github.io/papers/history.html
  pdf-url: https://tambapanni.github.io/papers/pdf/1. Tambapanni_History_Without_Borders.pdf
  doi: 10.5072/zenodo.471074
categories: [political-economy, state-building, historiography, sri-lanka]
abstract: |
  Every history is written through a lens. The question is whether the historian acknowledges the lens. The dominant historiography of the island known today as Sri Lanka, in antiquity Tambapanni to its earliest inhabitants, Taprobane to Greek and Roman traders, Serendib to Arab navigators, has been written through three overlapping lenses that systematically distort the evidence: the post-Westphalian nation-state framework, the Germanic Aryan philological tradition, and the colonial administrative need for fixed ethnic categories.
  
  This document does not claim to be free of perspective. It makes its perspective explicit, and follows two foundational principles: that the political problems of the island cannot be resolved without understanding the false historical foundation on which they have been built; and that understanding that foundation requires a framework for what a modern democratic state actually requires, and why the island has never had one.
---

## Part 1 of the Tambapanni Series

**Status:** Complete · v1.17 · March 2026

### Abstract

Every history is written through a lens. The question is whether the historian acknowledges the lens. The dominant historiography of the island known today as Sri Lanka, in antiquity Tambapanni to its earliest inhabitants, Taprobane to Greek and Roman traders, Serendib to Arab navigators, has been written through three overlapping lenses that systematically distort the evidence: the post-Westphalian nation-state framework, the Germanic Aryan philological tradition, and the colonial administrative need for fixed ethnic categories.

This document does not claim to be free of perspective. It makes its perspective explicit, and follows two foundational principles: that the political problems of the island cannot be resolved without understanding the false historical foundation on which they have been built; and that understanding that foundation requires a framework for what a modern democratic state actually requires, and why the island has never had one.

### Key Arguments

- **The Vijaya origin is a geography argument, not a culture argument.** The Mahavamsa's Lala points to the Gujarat/Rajasthan coast, not Bengal.

- **The Nayak kings disprove primordial exclusivism.** Four Telugu Hindu rulers became the greatest patrons of Sinhala Buddhism. The categories were socially real but not politically exclusivist.

- **Colonial administration manufactured fixed categories.** The 1871 census, Orientalist philology, and premature democratisation created the conditions for ethnic patronage competition.

- **The Sinhala Only Act is a sequencing failure, not ethnic hatred.** Pre-existing cultural difference was the fuel. Democratic competition without constitutional constraint was the ignition.

- **Dynastic competition was not ethnic warfare.** Chola, Polonnaruwa, and Magha episodes were political events within a connected civilisation, not invasions across an ethnic boundary.

### Frameworks Applied

- **Fukuyama** — Institutional sequencing: the impartial state must precede democratic accountability

### Downloads

[Download PDF](pdf/1.%20Tambapanni_History_Without_Borders.pdf){.btn .btn-primary} [DOI: 10.5072/zenodo.471074](https://doi.org/10.5072/zenodo.471074){.btn .btn-outline-secondary}

### Citation

Elampoornan, A. (2026). Tambapanni: A History Without Borders, v1.17. orcid.org/0009-0004-9181-2270

---

[← Back to series overview](../index.qmd)
